title: Heavy periods and excess bleeding
excerpt: In Ayurveda, periods that run too heavy or too long are called raktapradara, and the classical view ties them mostly to excess heat (pitta) in the blood. Cooling, calming food and rest are the everyday starting point.
body_html:
when your period feels like too much

Some months a period is just a few quiet days. Other times it soaks through pads faster than you can change them, drags on past a week, or comes with clots and a tiredness that lingers long after it ends. If that is you, you are not imagining it, and Ayurveda has been describing exactly this picture for a very long time.

The classical name is asrigdara or raktapradara. Broken down simply, rakta means blood and pradara means an excessive flow or a bursting forth, so the word itself means heavy menstrual bleeding (Madhava Nidana describes raktapradara among the disorders of the menstrual blood). Naming it matters, because it tells you this is a recognised condition with a known logic, not just bad luck.

rakta means blood and pradara means an excessive flow or a bursting forth, so the word itself means heavy menstrual bleeding

the pitta-and-heat view

Ayurveda sees menstrual blood (artava or raja) as closely tied to rakta, the blood tissue, which is governed largely by pitta, the principle of heat and transformation in the body. When pitta runs high, it heats and thins the blood and pushes it to flow more freely than it should. This is why the classical texts treat raktapradara mainly as a heat-driven, pitta-dominant problem (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana, in the section on rakta and bleeding disorders).

The everyday triggers the texts point to are the heating ones: too much sharp, sour, salty, fried and spicy food, alcohol, too much heat and sun, anger and stress, and overwork during the period itself (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, on pitta-aggravating diet and conduct). You may notice the bleeding is bright red, that you feel warm, irritable or thirsty, and that a fiery week of eating or tension makes the next period worse. That pattern is the heat showing itself.

Because heavy bleeding steadily drains the blood tissue, the texts also warn it can leave you pale, weak and short of breath over time, the picture of what we would now call anaemia (Madhava Nidana notes the weakness and loss that follow ongoing raktapradara). This is why the aim is not only to slow the flow but to protect your strength.

gentle cooling habits that help

The direction in the classical management is consistent and calm: cool the blood, soothe pitta, and rest. The remedies the texts lean on are sheeta (cooling) and astringent, the taste that gently tightens and holds (Chakradatta devotes a chapter to raktapradara built around cooling, astringent and blood-settling preparations).

On the plate, favour cooling, easy foods: rice, soft cooked vegetables, milk, ghee in modest amounts, and sweet ripe fruit like pomegranate, amla and figs, which the texts repeatedly use in bleeding conditions. Pull back during your period from chillies, deep-fried snacks, very sour pickles, excess salt, coffee and alcohol, all of which add heat. A classic, simple home measure is a teaspoon of amla or pomegranate, both prized in Ayurveda for settling heat in the blood.

Two coolers worth knowing: coriander, valued for cooling pitta and calming an overheated system, makes a gentle daily water (see recipe), and fennel, sweet and cooling, eases the cramping warmth that often rides along with heavy flow. Beyond food, the texts are firm about rest. Treat the period as a time to slow down: lighter work, earlier nights, less screen-fuelled stress, and keeping cool rather than hot. Anger and overexertion are named as aggravators, so the quiet is medicine too.

when to see a doctor

These gentle habits are for ordinary heavy periods, not a substitute for a diagnosis. Please see a doctor or a qualified vaidya without delay if you are soaking through a pad or tampon every hour for several hours, passing large clots, bleeding for more than seven days or between periods, bleeding after sex or after menopause, or feeling dizzy, breathless, very pale or unusually exhausted, which can signal anaemia. Sudden heavy bleeding with severe pain, or any bleeding in pregnancy, needs urgent care.

Heavy bleeding can have causes that need proper investigation, such as fibroids, thyroid issues, clotting problems or hormonal imbalance, so getting checked is wise rather than waiting it out. This article is general information drawn from classical Ayurvedic texts, not a personal diagnosis or a replacement for medical care.

Try This Today

  • keep the period plate cool: During heavy days, build meals around rice, soft cooked vegetables, milk and a little ghee, and skip chillies, deep-fried snacks, sour pickles, excess salt, coffee and alcohol, which add heat.
  • a spoon of pomegranate or amla: A teaspoon of pomegranate or amla, fresh or as juice, is a classic, gentle home measure for settling heat in the blood. Add ripe figs and pomegranate seeds as everyday cooling fruit.
  • rest like it is medicine: The texts name anger and overexertion as aggravators. Treat the period as a slow-down: lighter work, earlier nights, less heat and stress, and keeping yourself cool rather than overheated.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Coriander (धनिया)

Cooling and calming for pitta; a gentle daily coriander water is a simple way to settle heat in the blood.

Fennel (सौंफ)

Sweet and cooling; eases the cramping warmth that often accompanies heavy flow.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: In Ayurveda, periods that run too heavy or too long are called raktapradara, and the classical view ties them mostly to excess heat (pitta) in the blood. Cooling, calming food and rest are the everyday starting point.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Asrigdara or raktapradara is the classical name for heavy or prolonged menstrual bleeding, seen mainly as a heat (pitta) problem in the blood.
Cool the blood and calm pitta: favour rice, milk, ghee, pomegranate and amla; cut chillies, fried, sour and salty food, coffee and alcohol during periods.
See a doctor for very heavy or prolonged flow, large clots, bleeding between periods, or signs of anaemia like dizziness and breathlessness.

glossary_terms.definition: The classical Ayurvedic term for heavy or excessive menstrual bleeding; rakta means blood, pradara an excessive flow.
The blood tissue, governed largely by pitta; menstrual blood is seen as closely tied to it.
Menstrual blood and the menstrual flow in Ayurvedic physiology.
The dosha of heat and transformation; when raised, it heats and thins the blood and drives excess flow.
Cooling in quality; the property of foods and remedies favoured to calm heat-driven bleeding.

sources: Madhava Nidana, section on menstrual blood disorders (asrigdara / raktapradara)
Caraka Samhita, on the principle that pitta governs rakta and on bleeding disorders (raktapitta)
Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, on pitta-aggravating diet and conduct
Chakradatta, section on raktapradara (cooling and astringent treatment)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Raktapradara / Asrigdara" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Raktapradara / Asrigdara is defined as: The classical Ayurvedic term for heavy or excessive menstrual bleeding; rakta means blood, pradara an excessive flow.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to keep the period plate cool. During heavy days, build meals around rice, soft cooked vegetables, milk and a little ghee, and skip chillies, deep-fried snacks, sour pickles, excess salt, coffee and alcohol, which add heat.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Coriander for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Coriander is highly regarded. Cooling and calming for pitta; a gentle daily coriander water is a simple way to settle heat in the blood.
